diary: Saturday started gray but gentle. I caught the early light filtering through my apartment in Ravenna, showered off the week, and headed to Fremont with my canvas tote. The farmers market was alive—vendors calling out, families weaving between stalls. I grabbed fresh berries and some bread that smelled like home. The commute to Kirkland felt longer than usual, maybe because I knew the bookstore would be busy with people ducking in from the Mariners crowds down in SoDo. Sure enough, we had a steady stream all afternoon. A teenager came in looking for poetry; I spent twenty minutes helping her find exactly what she needed. Lunch was quiet—just me, a sandwich, and my thoughts. By evening I was back in my studio, paintbrush in hand, letting the day drain out through my fingers. Salsa footwork after dinner felt good. The overcast sky never cleared, but somehow that made everything feel cozier.
thought_process: I started the day wanting to connect with my neighborhood—the farmers market felt like the right move on an overcast Saturday. Work at the bookstore was steady but not overwhelming, which let me be present with customers rather than stressed. I noticed I gravitated toward solitude after hours: painting, reading, dancing alone. Maybe I needed that decompression after a week of being 'on' for customers. The Mariners game happening nearby created ambient energy in the city, but I stayed in my quieter orbit. By evening I was content, not restless. The gray weather suited my mood—no FOMO about missing sunshine, just permission to be introspective.
world_event_prompt: Overcast Saturday, high of 64F. Farmers markets in Ballard, Fremont, and the U-District in full swing. Mariners home game at T-Mobile Park at 1pm brings SoDo crowds.
